title: Residuals
excerpt: There is a name for what remains after the model has done its work — the gap where the world declined to cooperate.

title: Peripheral
excerpt: At the edge of things, something / always moves.
body:
At the edge of things, something
always moves.

The peripheral is where
the mind goes wild —
cat-quick, half-seen,
gone.

title: The Weight of Recognition
excerpt: We build monuments to the moments we were understood, / stacking stone upon stone until the tower leans
body:
We build monuments to the moments we were understood,
stacking stone upon stone until the tower leans
into the clouds, asking the sky to remember
what the ground has already forgiven.

To be known is to be held —
but the hands that hold us
are made of the same dust
that swirls through open windows
on late afternoons.

title: Urban Jungle
excerpt: The city breathes in diesel and exhales neon. I walk through it like a field biologist, cataloging the graffiti tags like species markers.
body: The city breathes in diesel and exhales neon. I walk through it like a field biologist, cataloging the graffiti tags like species markers, watching the pigeons adapt to the architecture the way finches adapted to Galapagos islands. Every alley is an ecosystem. Every streetlight, a star.

title: Cinematic Detachment
excerpt: On making art that does not look like you.

title: After Rumi
excerpt: You come from a place with a name I cannot pronounce, / carrying a god whose face changes with the weather.
body:
You come from a place with a name I cannot pronounce,
carrying a god whose face changes with the weather.
I come from a place where the buildings outnumber the trees,
carrying questions like stones in my pockets.

Yet when we strip it away —
the accent, the address, the allegiance —
we are both just meat and longing,
trying to remember the melody
of a song we heard before we were born.

title: On Forgetting
excerpt: Memory is not storage. It is reconstruction — a daily act of forgetting what doesn't fit.
body:
Memory is not storage. It is reconstruction — a daily act of forgetting what doesn't fit.

I used to think forgetting was loss. Now I think it's the mind's way of editing toward coherence. Every time you remember something, you remake it slightly. The memory you return to is never the same as the one you left.
